frontmatter.title: Bicategories are virtual bicategories with all composites

mainmatter.p: A bicategory is the same thing as a virtual bicategory that has all composites, as observed by Derek Elkins.
A directed graph G is defined to be a collection of 0-cells A,B,C, \ldots \in G together with a collection of edges { A } \xrightarrow{e} { B } \in G.

frontmatter.title: Free category on a directed graph

mainmatter.p: Let G be a directed graph; a path in G is defined to be a sequence of compatible edges { \left [ A_0 \xrightarrow{f_0} A_1 \xrightarrow{f_1} \ldots \xrightarrow{f_{n-1}} A_n \right ]}. Evidently, the free category G ^{ \star } on G has morphisms given by paths.
A virtual bicategory \mathcal{K} is a directed graph equipped with the following additional data:

ol.li: for each path { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and each edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \in \mathcal{K}, a collection of 2-cells { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A,B \right )};
for any edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B }, an identity 2-cell { { \left [ f \right ]} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ f }} { f } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A,B \right )};

for any sequence of compatible paths A_0 \xrightarrow{\Psi _0} \ldots \xrightarrow{\Psi _{n-1}} A_n \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and 2-cells { \Psi _i } \xrightarrow{\alpha _i} { f_i } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A_i,A_{i+1} \right )} and { { \left [ f_i \mid i<n \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\beta} { g } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A_0,A_n \right )}, a composite 2-cell { \Psi _0; \ldots ; \Psi _n } \xrightarrow{{ \left [ \alpha _i \mid i<n \right ]} ; \beta} { g };
such that the composition of 2-cells is both associative and unital with respect to identity 2-cells.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory; given a path { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and an edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } is called cocartesian if any 2-cell { \Phi ; \Psi ; \Xi } \xrightarrow{\beta} { s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { \Phi ; { \left [ f \right ]} ; \Xi } \xrightarrow{\beta \setminus \alpha} { s } such that \beta = { \left [ \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Phi _i } \ldots} , \alpha , \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Xi _i } \ldots} \right ]} ; { \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right )}.

frontmatter.title: Left- and right-cocartesian 2-cells in a virtual bicategory

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory; given a path { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and an edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } is called left-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Psi ; \Xi } \xrightarrow{\beta} { s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { { \left [ f \right ]} ; \Psi } \xrightarrow{\beta \setminus \alpha} { s } such that \beta = { \left [ \alpha , \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Xi _i } \ldots} \right ]} ; { \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right )}.

Dually, given a path { B } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { C } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and an edge { B } \xrightarrow{f} { C }, a 2-cell { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } is called right-cocartesian if for 2-cell { \Phi ; \Psi } \xrightarrow{\beta} { s } factors uniquely through \alpha in the sense that there exists a unique 2-cell { \Phi ; { \left [ f \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\beta \setminus \alpha} { s } such that \beta = { \left [ \overrightarrow{1 _{ \Phi _i } \ldots} , \alpha \right ]} ; { \left ( \beta \setminus \alpha \right )}.
These definitions are due to Arkor and McDermott.
Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory, and let { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } be a path in \mathcal{K}. Then we define:

ol.li: a composite of \Psi is a cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a left half-composite of \Psi is a left-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a right half-composite of \Psi is a right-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi.

frontmatter.title: Bicategories are virtual bicategories with all composites

mainmatter.p: A bicategory is the same thing as a virtual bicategory that has all composites, as observed by Derek Elkins.

I still don't know if the following are the correct definitions. One would have to check that the left-composite of a pair of maps is isomorphic to the right-composite if both exist, which sounds plausible.

frontmatter.title: Thunkable and linear maps in a virtual bicategory

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory; we isolate two classes of edges:

ol.li: an edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } in \mathcal{K} is called thunkable if and only if for any edge { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } there exists a left half-composite { { \left [ f;g \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\lambda} { h };

dually, an edge { B } \xrightarrow{g} { C } in \mathcal{K} is called linear if and only if for any edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } there exists a right half-composite { { \left [ f;g \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\rho} { h }.

mainmatter.p: Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory; we isolate two classes of objects in \mathcal{K}, by analogy with the case for deductive systems:

ol.li: an object B \in \mathcal{K} is called positive when every edge { B } \xrightarrow{f} { C } is linear;
an object B \in \mathcal{K} is called negative when every edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } is thunkable.

mainmatter.p: Then we say that an object is polarized when it is either positive or negative in the above sense.
Let \mathcal{K} be a virtual bicategory, and let { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } be a path in \mathcal{K}. Then we define:

ol.li: a composite of \Psi is a cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a left half-composite of \Psi is a left-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi;
a right half-composite of \Psi is a right-cocartesian 2-cell with domain \Psi.

mainmatter.p: A virtual bicategory \mathcal{K} is a directed graph equipped with the following additional data:

ol.li: for each path { A } \xrightarrow{\Psi} { B } \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and each edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B } \in \mathcal{K}, a collection of 2-cells { \Psi } \xrightarrow{\alpha} { f } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A,B \right )};
for any edge { A } \xrightarrow{f} { B }, an identity 2-cell { { \left [ f \right ]} } \xrightarrow{1 _{ f }} { f } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A,B \right )};

for any sequence of compatible paths A_0 \xrightarrow{\Psi _0} \ldots \xrightarrow{\Psi _{n-1}} A_n \in \mathcal{K} ^{ \star } and 2-cells { \Psi _i } \xrightarrow{\alpha _i} { f_i } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A_i,A_{i+1} \right )} and { { \left [ f_i \mid i<n \right ]} } \xrightarrow{\beta} { g } \in \mathcal{K} { \left ( A_0,A_n \right )}, a composite 2-cell { \Psi _0; \ldots ; \Psi _n } \xrightarrow{{ \left [ \alpha _i \mid i<n \right ]} ; \beta} { g };
such that the composition of 2-cells is both associative and unital with respect to identity 2-cells.

mainmatter.p: A bicategory B is given by the following structure and axioms:

ol.li: a collection of objects x,y,z called 0-cells;
for each pair of 0-cells x,y a category \mathbf{hom} _{ B } { \left ( x , y \right )} of morphisms or 1-cells x \xrightarrow{f} y;
for each object x, a morphism x \xrightarrow{1 _{ x }} x called the identity morphism;

for all objects x,y,z, a functor \mathbf{hom} _{ B } { \left ( x , y \right )} \times \mathbf{hom} _{ B } { \left ( y , z \right )} \xrightarrow{{ \left ( \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} \right )}} \mathbf{hom} _{ B } { \left ( x , z \right )} called horizontal composition;

for all 1-morphisms e \xrightarrow{f} x \xrightarrow{g} y \xrightarrow{h} z, an isomorphism { \left ( f \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} g \right )} \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} h \xrightarrow{\alpha _{ f,g,h }} f \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} { \left ( g \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} h \right )} natural in f,g,h called the associator;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow{f} y, an isomorphism 1 _{ x } \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} f \xrightarrow{\lambda _{ f }} f natural in f called the left unitor;
for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow{f} y, an isomorphism f \mathbin{\boldsymbol{;}} 1 _{ y } \xrightarrow{\rho _{ f }} f natural in f called the right unitor;
such that the triangle below commutes for all 1-morphisms x \xrightarrow{f} y \xrightarrow{g} z:
and the pentagon below commutes for all 1-morphisms v \xrightarrow{e} w \xrightarrow{f} x \xrightarrow{g} y \xrightarrow{h} z:
